metadata.summary: Layered security testing using SAST, DAST, SCA, and secret detection at every CI/CD stage. SAST and secrets on every PR, DAST nightly on staging, container scan on image build.

content.title: Security Testing
content.summary: Layered security testing using SAST (static code analysis), DAST (dynamic attack simulation), SCA (dependency vulnerability scanning), and secret detection — integrated at every stage of the CI/CD pipeline. Every PR must pass SAST and secrets checks; DAST runs nightly on staging; container scans run on image build.

why.p: Attackers exploit known vulnerability patterns (OWASP Top 10, API Security Top 10) that automated tools can detect before deployment. Shift-left integration (SAST on every commit, secrets pre-commit) reduces fix cost by orders of magnitude compared to post-production discovery. Tools do not replace threat modeling or manual penetration testing — they triage known patterns so humans focus on logic flaws.

list.item: Every change to auth, authorization, input handling, file upload, deserialization, crypto, or session code
Pre-launch on new services before internet exposure
Continuous: SAST + secret scanning on every PR, SCA on every build, DAST nightly on staging
New dependency adoption (SCA before merge)
Incident response to confirm scope and regression-test the fix
Compliance audits (SOC 2, ISO 27001, PCI)
As a substitute for threat modeling — tools find known patterns; design flaws hide from scanners
DAST against any environment with real user data, real billing, or real downstream effects
Replacing manual review for critical-path code (auth, crypto, payment)
Auto-merging security patches without human review

section.name: pipeline-and-rules
section.p: Defines where each security testing category integrates in the CI/CD pipeline and the gate criteria that block or warn.

rule.statement: Run SAST (Semgrep, Bandit) and secret scanning (TruffleHog, Gitleaks) on every commit and every PR — these are fast (<2 min) and catch the majority of static vulnerabilities.
rule.rationale: Every commit is an attack surface change. Fast gates keep feedback immediate; deferred scanning gives vulnerabilities days to propagate.

rule.statement: Run SCA (Snyk, pip-audit, npm audit) on every build. Block on Critical CVEs or CVEs known to be exploited in the wild; warn on High CVEs with no fix available.
rule.rationale: Dependencies change on their own schedule. Every build is a new dependency risk surface. Critical exploitable CVEs are production emergencies.

rule.statement: Run DAST (OWASP ZAP, Nuclei) nightly on staging only — never against production or any environment with real user data or billing.
rule.rationale: DAST simulates attacks; running against production data causes real side effects. Staging must be isolated.

rule.statement: Run container scanning (Trivy) on every image build. Block on CRITICAL/HIGH severity unfixed vulnerabilities in the image.
rule.rationale: Container base images accumulate CVEs silently. Scanning at build time catches regressions before they reach the registry.

rule.statement: Define a baseline for pre-existing findings. Only new findings (relative to the baseline) block PRs. Pre-existing findings receive a triage decision once and move to an allowlist or backlog.
rule.rationale: Inheriting thousands of existing findings creates alert fatigue. New findings deserve immediate attention; legacy findings get tracked separately.

rule.statement: Any secret detected by TruffleHog or Gitleaks must block the PR with no exceptions. The secret must be revoked and rotated immediately — removal from git does not expire a leaked secret.
rule.rationale: Secrets pushed to git history are compromised forever. Rotation is the only mitigation; history scrubbing is secondary cleanup.

rule.statement: Add pre-commit hooks for Gitleaks and detect-secrets in every project. Developers should not be able to commit secrets accidentally.
rule.rationale: Pre-commit hooks catch the mistake before the git object exists, before the secret is ever visible in the remote.

rule.statement: Run threat modeling on new features before any tool touches them. Tools find known patterns; design flaws hide from scanners.
rule.rationale: BOLA, IDOR, and business logic abuse are the top API risk categories and are invisible to automated scanning.

section.name: tool-categories
section.p: SAST, DAST, SCA, and secret-detection tools with their intended use context and key CLI commands.

rule.statement: SAST tools (Semgrep, Bandit, CodeQL, Snyk Code) analyze source code without executing the application. Run on every commit and PR.
rule.rationale: Static analysis is fast and catches the broadest range of code-level vulnerabilities. Multi-language tools like Semgrep enable polyglot coverage.

list.item: Semgrep — multi-language, custom rules, fast CI integration; semgrep --config p/owasp-top-ten .
Bandit — Python-specific security patterns; bandit -r src/ -f json -o bandit-report.json
CodeQL — deep dataflow analysis, GitHub-native; gh extension install github/gh-codeql
Snyk Code — developer-friendly, PR feedback, reachability analysis

rule.statement: DAST tools (OWASP ZAP, Nuclei, Burp Suite, StackHawk) test running applications by simulating attacks. Use on staging environments only — never against production or any environment with real user data or billing.
rule.rationale: Dynamic testing finds runtime vulnerabilities and configuration issues that static analysis misses. Staging isolation prevents accidental data mutation.

rule.statement: Provide DAST tools with an authenticated session via a custom auth script — unauthenticated DAST misses 80% of the attack surface.
rule.rationale: Most application logic sits behind authentication. Unauthenticated scans find only public endpoints and misconfiguration.

list.item: OWASP ZAP — open source, scriptable, CI-friendly; docker run zaproxy/zap-stable zap-baseline.py -t https://staging.example.com
Nuclei — template-based scanning; nuclei -u https://staging.example.com -t cves/
Burp Suite — manual penetration testing, deep analysis
StackHawk — DAST in CI with hawk CLI and IaC support

rule.statement: SCA tools (Snyk, Trivy, pip-audit, npm audit, osv-scanner) scan dependencies for known CVEs and license issues. Run on every build.
rule.rationale: Third-party dependencies are attack vectors. Every build introduces new vulnerabilities from upstream releases.

rule.statement: Use reachability analysis (Snyk Code, Semgrep Pro) to distinguish exploitable CVEs from unreachable transitive dependencies. CVSS score alone is a poor triage signal.
rule.rationale: SCA generates high noise from transitive deps. A CVSS-10 in unreachable code is lower priority than a CVSS-6 in a public endpoint.

list.item: Snyk — continuous monitoring, auto-fix PRs, reachability; npm i -g snyk
Trivy — containers, IaC, SBOM; brew install trivy
pip-audit / safety — Python packages; pip install pip-audit
npm audit — Node.js packages, bundled with npm
osv-scanner — Google OSV vulnerability database; brew install osv-scanner

rule.statement: Secret detection tools (TruffleHog, Gitleaks, detect-secrets, GitHub Secret Scanning) find hardcoded credentials, API keys, and tokens in source code and git history. Run on every commit with pre-commit hooks.
rule.rationale: Secrets in git are compromised forever. Pre-commit detection prevents them from entering the repository in the first place.

list.item: TruffleHog — entropy + patterns, 700+ detectors, verified mode; trufflehog git file://. --only-verified
Gitleaks — regex patterns, fast, pre-commit hook; gitleaks detect --source . --verbose
detect-secrets — baseline methodology, low false positives; pip install detect-secrets
GitHub Secret Scanning — native to GitHub, free for public repos

section.name: owasp-top-10-and-api-security
section.p: Current OWASP Web and API risk rankings with test approaches for auth and authorization vulnerabilities.

rule.statement: For every authenticated endpoint, test: correct credentials (200), missing Authorization header (401), expired token (401), insufficient role (403), and another user's resource ID (403 or 404 — must not be 200).
rule.rationale: Horizontal privilege escalation (user A accessing user B's data) is the #1 API bug class and requires explicit per-endpoint testing.

list.item: A01 Broken Access Control — #1 risk; includes SSRF consolidated from 2021. Test authorization at every function level.
A02 Security Misconfiguration — moved up from #5; debug modes, default credentials, unnecessary features enabled.
A03 Software Supply Chain Failures — NEW; extends beyond dependencies to build systems and distribution infrastructure.
A05 Injection — SQL, NoSQL, OS command, LDAP, XSS. Use parameterized queries; never concatenate user input.
A07 Authentication Failures — weak passwords, session management, credential stuffing.
A10 Mishandling Exceptional Conditions — NEW; improper error handling, failing open, logical errors.
API1 Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA) — access other users' objects by changing IDs in requests.
API2 Broken Authentication — token manipulation, session attacks.
API3 Broken Object Property Level Authorization (BOPLA) — access restricted properties via mass assignment.
API4 Unrestricted Resource Consumption — rate limiting, payload size limits.
API5 Broken Function Level Authorization — admin endpoint access, privilege escalation.
API7 Server Side Request Forgery — internal network access via API parameters.
API9 Improper Inventory Management — shadow APIs, undocumented endpoints.

rule.statement: BOLA (API1) and BOPLA (API3) require human review — scanners cannot detect them because the vulnerability is in business logic, not code patterns.
rule.rationale: Automated tools look for code signatures. BOLA exploits are valid HTTP requests using different IDs — indistinguishable from legitimate traffic to a scanner.

example.title: Injection test matrix — minimum payloads
example.code: SQL: ' OR '1'='1 ;DROP TABLE users-- NoSQL: {"$gt": ""} {"$ne": null} Command: ; ls | cat /etc/passwd XSS: <script>alert(1)</script> Path: ../../../etc/passwd XXE: <!DOCTYPE x [<!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd">]>

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.p: Critical failure modes when AI agents interact with security tooling, and best practices for secure-by-default pipeline operation.

antipattern.description: Agent widens allowlists or suppresses SAST rules to make findings go away
antipattern.reason: Suppression hides real vulnerabilities. Lock .semgrepignore and .snyk behind a separate review path; require a one-line rationale and review-by date on every suppression.

antipattern.description: Agent writes LLM-generated regex for input validation
antipattern.reason: LLM-generated regex is famously bypassable. Use battle-tested libraries (bleach, defusedxml, validators, email-validator) and pin versions.

antipattern.description: Agent auto-merges Dependabot/Snyk PRs without integration tests
antipattern.reason: Dependency updates break prod weekly without a green build + integration test gate on the changed dependency.

antipattern.description: Agent hallucinates CVE IDs and remediation steps from training data
antipattern.reason: Always cross-check findings against NVD (nvd.nist.gov) or osv.dev before acting.

antipattern.description: Agent generates crypto primitives ("here's a custom AES-GCM wrapper")
antipattern.reason: Custom crypto is the most dangerous code class. Force cryptography.fernet, libsodium, or KMS-managed keys. Reject ad-hoc primitives.

antipattern.description: Agent runs DAST automation against production URLs
antipattern.reason: DAST scripts that mutate prod data cause real side effects. Restrict to non-prod URLs in CI policy and by ACL.

antipattern.description: Agent suppresses CodeQL/Semgrep with `// nosec` without a rationale
antipattern.reason: CI must lint commits for naked suppressions. Every suppression requires a one-line reason.

antipattern.description: Secret rotation requires only one of three coordinated changes (key in vault, app config, dependent services)
antipattern.reason: An agent doing only one of three creates an outage. Stage rotation in a runbook the agent follows step-by-step.

rule.statement: Define a triage process before connecting any scanner to CI. Pre-existing findings get one triage decision; only new findings block PRs.
rule.rationale: High false-positive rates from DAST and SCA erode trust in the pipeline. A triage process prevents "turn it off" reactions.

rule.statement: Sign artifacts (cosign/sigstore), pin base images by digest, generate SBOM (syft), and scan the SBOM (grype) as part of every container build.
rule.rationale: Supply chain attacks (OWASP A03 2025) target build artifacts and base images. Signing + SBOM provides provenance verification.

rule.statement: Human-in-loop on every Critical finding. Agents may auto-open PRs for Low/Medium findings with regression tests; Critical findings require human review before merge.
rule.rationale: The business and legal consequences of Critical vulnerabilities require human judgment about risk acceptance, timing, and disclosure.

rule.statement: Run penetration testing annually and after major architectural changes. Automated scanners do not replace pentesters for logic flaws and novel attack chains.
rule.rationale: Scanners find what they were programmed to find. Pentesters find what attackers find — including combinations of low-severity findings that chain into critical exploits.

template.purpose: GitHub Actions security workflow (SAST + secrets + SCA + container)
Agent prompt: triage scanner JSON by reachability, open fix PR
Agent prompt: audit routes for OWASP API Top 10 issues
